title: Tres leches cake
subtitle: Sponge saturated with condensed, evaporated, and double cream
excerpt: A light sponge baked in a rectangular tin, soaked immediately while warm with a three-milk mixture of condensed milk, evaporated milk, and double cream, then refrigerated until fully saturated. The crumb becomes cold and custardy throughout rather than wet only on the surface. Served chilled with a layer of softly whipped cream.
sourceNotes: Tres leches cake has roots across several Latin American countries including Mexico, Nicaragua, and Cuba, with early published recipes appearing from the 1940s onward on Nestle and Carnation evaporated and condensed milk tins distributed across the region. The technique of soaking a sponge with sweetened milk draws on older European baking traditions carried across by 19th-century immigration. The recipe here uses a separated-egg sponge (yolks beaten with sugar, whites folded in) for a light, porous crumb that absorbs the soak without becoming stodgy.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake must be made at least 4 hours ahead; overnight is better. Add the whipped cream topping on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. The sponge bakes pale golden; it should not colour deeply. The soak goes on while the sponge is still warm in the tin.

recipeTools.notes: 30 × 20 cm rectangular tin, greased and lined

glossaryTerms.definition: The defining technique of tres leches: a mixture of sweetened condensed milk, evaporated milk, and double cream poured over a warm sponge immediately after baking. The sponge is pierced all over with a skewer first to create channels for the liquid. As the cake cools to room temperature and then chills in the fridge, the crumb absorbs the soak throughout, becoming cold and custardy rather than wet only on the surface. The ratio of the three milks determines the sweetness and richness of the finished cake.

content.text: goes on while the sponge is warm and still in the tin. Pierce the surface thoroughly with a skewer before pouring; you need channels for the liquid to travel into the crumb, not surface puddles. Pour the soak slowly in three stages, letting each addition absorb before adding the next. The sponge will look wet at this stage; cover the tin and refrigerate. It firms and becomes custardy as it chills overnight.

attrs.prepNote: 150 g for the yolks, 50 g for the whites
to dust over the finished cake

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Grease and line a 30 × 20 cm rectangular tin. Sift together the flour, baking powder, and salt. Beat the egg yolks with 150 g caster sugar until pale and thick. Beat in the vanilla and milk. Fold in the flour mixture. In a separate clean bowl, whisk the egg whites to soft peaks, then whisk in the remaining 50 g caster sugar until stiff and glossy. Fold a third of the whites into the yolk batter to loosen it, then fold in the rest in two additions. Pour into the prepared tin and bake for 25 to 30 minutes until a skewer comes out clean.

Leave the sponge in the tin. Prick the surface all over with a skewer. Whisk together the condensed milk, evaporated milk, and 200 ml double cream. Pour the soak over the warm sponge in three slow additions, allowing each to be absorbed before adding the next. Cover the tin and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or overnight.
When ready to serve, whip the 300 ml double cream with the icing sugar to soft peaks. Spread over the chilled cake and dust lightly with cinnamon. Cut into portions in the tin and serve cold.
